Learning Objective 41H — Embryology

Development of the Face & Palate

Including clinical presentations of cleft lip and cleft palate — based on lecture transcript, Moore's Developing Human, and Langman's Medical Embryology

Overview Facial Prominences Nasal Development Primary Palate Secondary Palate Histology Cleft Lip Cleft Palate Associated Syndromes MCQs
Section 01

Overview of Facial Development

The face develops between weeks 4–10 of embryonic development, primarily from five facial prominences that surround the primitive mouth (stomodeum). These prominences are composed of neural crest–derived mesenchyme covered by ectoderm. Their fusion and merging create the definitive facial features. Simultaneously, internal cavities — the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and paranasal sinuses — are partitioned and refined.

Key Concept
The five facial prominences arise from the frontonasal prominence (unpaired, gives rise to forehead, nose, philtrum) and from bilateral maxillary and mandibular prominences (derivatives of the 1st pharyngeal arch). Neural crest cell migration into these prominences is essential; defective migration underlies many facial syndromes.
Week 4
Stomodeum (primitive mouth) forms; frontonasal prominence prominent above it. Nasal placodes appear as ectodermal thickenings.
Week 5
Nasal pits invaginate from nasal placodes; medial and lateral nasal prominences form around pits. Maxillary prominences grow medially.
Weeks 6–7
Medial nasal prominences merge with each other and with maxillary prominences → forms upper lip and primary palate. Nasal sac deepens.
Week 7–8
Oronasal membrane ruptures. Lateral palatine processes begin forming from maxillary prominences, initially oriented vertically.
Weeks 8–10
Lateral palatine processes elevate to horizontal, fuse with each other and with the nasal septum → secondary palate (hard + soft palate + uvula) complete by ~week 12.
Section 02

The Five Facial Prominences

All facial structures derive from mesenchyme-filled swellings called prominences. Merging = fusion across a midline groove with no residual trace. Fusion = contact of two epithelial surfaces followed by mesenchymal breakthrough.

Prominence Origin Contributions
Frontonasal prominence Proliferating mesenchyme ventral to forebrain Forehead, dorsum & apex of nose, primary palate (via medial nasal prominences)
Medial nasal prominences (×2) Medial walls of nasal pits (from frontonasal) Philtrum of upper lip, premaxilla (supports incisors), primary palate
Lateral nasal prominences (×2) Lateral walls of nasal pits (from frontonasal) Alae of nose
Maxillary prominences (×2) 1st pharyngeal arch (dorsal part) Cheeks, lateral upper lip, secondary palate (palatine processes), most of maxilla
Mandibular prominences (×2) 1st pharyngeal arch (ventral part) Lower lip, chin, mandible
Nasolacrimal Groove
A groove forms between the lateral nasal and maxillary prominences. Ectoderm in this groove sinks → forms the nasolacrimal duct and lacrimal sac. Failure = epiphora (overflow tearing).
Section 03

Nasal Cavity Development

The nasal placode is an ectodermal thickening on the frontonasal prominence. Surrounding mesenchyme proliferates, forming a horseshoe-shaped ridge that creates the nasal pit. The nasal pit deepens into a nasal sac and is initially separated from the oral cavity by the oronasal membrane.

Key Events

Oronasal Membrane Rupture (~week 6)

The oronasal membrane separates the nasal sac from the stomodeum. As the nasal sac deepens, this membrane ruptures, creating the primitive choanae and establishing continuity between nasal and oral cavities. The nasal septum and palate will later re-separate these spaces.

Nasal Septum

The nasal septum grows inferiorly from the fused medial nasal prominences. It descends and fuses with the forming hard palate (completed after week 10), finally separating left and right nasal passages.

Nasal Conchae

The inferior, middle, and superior conchae arise as elevations from the lateral nasal cavity wall. They increase surface area for air conditioning and filtration.

Paranasal Sinuses

Develop postnatally as outgrowths (evaginations) of the nasal cavity mucosa into adjacent bones: maxillary, ethmoid, sphenoid, and frontal sinuses. All are pneumatized extensions from the nasal cavity.

Section 04

Development of the Primary Palate

The primary palate (median palatal process / intermaxillary segment) is a small, wedge-shaped structure anterior to the incisive foramen. It forms from the merger of the two medial nasal prominences with each other and then with the maxillary prominences.

What the Primary Palate Supports
The primary palate supports the upper central and lateral incisors. The philtrum (the vertical groove of the upper lip) is formed from the skin overlying the fused medial nasal prominences. The frenulum of the upper lip anchors this segment to the gingiva.

The primary palate is small — it constitutes only the premaxillary part of the adult hard palate, anterior to the incisive foramen. Everything posterior to the incisive foramen is derived from the secondary palate.

Section 05

Development of the Secondary Palate

The secondary palate forms from paired lateral palatine processes that grow from the maxillary prominences. It constitutes the bulk of the adult hard palate and all of the soft palate and uvula.

Stages of Secondary Palate Formation

1. Vertical orientation (weeks 6–7): The lateral palatine processes initially grow downward on each side of the developing tongue, oriented vertically.

2. Horizontal elevation (~week 7–8): As the head lifts and the tongue descends, the lateral palatine processes abruptly "elevate" to a horizontal orientation, extending toward the midline.

3. Fusion (weeks 8–12): The two lateral palatine processes fuse with each other at the midline (forming the palatine raphe). Fusion also occurs anteriorly with the primary palate, and superiorly with the descending nasal septum. Fusion proceeds anterior → posterior.

4. Bony ossification: The anterior portion ossifies to form the hard palate; the posterior portion remains muscular as the soft palate. The midline posterior projection becomes the uvula.

Sexual Dimorphism in Palate Fusion
Lateral palatine processes fuse approximately 1 week later in females than in males. This explains the higher incidence of isolated cleft palate in females — the longer window of vulnerability to teratogens or fusion failure.
StructureEmbryological OriginAdult Structure
Medial nasal prominences (fused)Frontonasal prominencePrimary palate (premaxilla) — anterior to incisive foramen; supports incisors
Lateral palatine processes (fused)Maxillary prominence (1st arch)Secondary palate — hard palate (ossified) + soft palate + uvula
Nasal septum (descends)Fused medial nasal prominencesNasal septum — fuses superiorly with hard palate
Section 06

Histology of Palate Development

Histological sections through the developing head confirm the sequence of palate formation. Key landmarks visible in sections are: the nasal septum (NS), the tongue (T), and the two palatine processes (P).

Section 07

Cleft Lip

Cleft lip results from failure of the maxillary prominence to fuse with the medial nasal prominence. A persistent labial groove (furrow) between these structures that fails to be filled by invading mesenchyme leads to clefting.

Pathogenesis

Normally, the groove between the medial nasal and maxillary prominences is obliterated by mesodermal proliferation from beneath. If the mesenchyme fails to fill this groove — due to hypoplasia of the maxillary prominence — the overlying epithelium breaks down (apoptosis of the thinned epithelium) and a cleft results.

Clinical Features — Cleft Lip
Incidence~1 in 1,000 live births
SexMore common in males
LateralityUnilateral (left > right) or bilateral
AssociationMay occur with or without cleft palate; can be isolated
CauseHypoplasia of maxillary prominence; failure of mesenchymal penetration
SeverityRanges from notch in vermillion to complete cleft through upper lip and upper jaw (between incisors and canines)

Classification

Incomplete: a notch or groove in the upper lip only, without extension to the nostril. Complete: full-thickness cleft extending through lip, alveolus, and floor of nose. Bilateral complete cleft lip creates a prominent premaxillary segment (the philtrum and primary palate) projecting forward.

Cleft Lip vs. Cleft Palate — Independence
Cleft lip and cleft palate involve different structures and different developmental events. They can occur independently or together. Isolated cleft lip is more common in males; isolated cleft palate is more common in females.
Section 08

Cleft Palate

Cleft palate results from failure of fusion of the lateral palatine processes with each other, with the primary palate, and/or with the descending nasal septum. Because the palatine processes are derived separately from the maxillary prominences, cleft palate can occur independently of cleft lip.

Pathogenesis

Any factor that prevents the lateral palatine processes from elevating to a horizontal position or that interferes with their midline fusion can cause cleft palate. The posterior soft palate is the last region to fuse and is therefore the most vulnerable. Cleft uvula represents the mildest form.

Clinical Features — Cleft Palate
Incidence~1 in 2,500 live births (less common than cleft lip ± palate)
SexMore common in females — due to later fusion of palatine processes (~1 week)
LocationPrimary palate cleft (anterior to incisive foramen) vs. secondary palate cleft (posterior); can be both
TeratogensAnticonvulsants (phenytoin, valproate), corticosteroids, alcohol, smoking
ChromosomalTrisomy 13 (Patau), Trisomy 18; various deletion syndromes
Functional consequencesFeeding difficulties (poor suction), nasal regurgitation, speech problems (hypernasal voice), recurrent otitis media (Eustachian tube dysfunction), dental anomalies

Pre- and Postnatal Detection

Cleft Lip vs. Cleft Palate — Key Comparison
Cleft Lip (± palate)Isolated Cleft Palate
Incidence1 : 1,0001 : 2,500
Sex predominanceMaleFemale
Structures involvedMedial nasal + maxillary prominencesLateral palatine processes ± primary palate
CauseFailure of mesodermal penetration of labial grooveFailure of palatine process elevation/fusion
LandmarkAnterior to incisive foramenPosterior to incisive foramen (secondary palate)
Section 09

Associated Syndromes

Treacher Collins Syndrome (1st Arch Syndrome)

Also known as mandibulofacial dysostosis. Caused by defective neural crest cell migration into the first pharyngeal arch.

Treacher Collins — Features
  • Zygomatic hypoplasia (flat cheeks)
  • Hypoplasia of mandibular ramus
  • Antimongoloid slant of palpebral fissures
  • Colobomas of lateral 1/3 of lower eyelid
  • Malformed pinna / external auditory canal
  • Obliteration of fronto-nasal angle
  • Large mouth (macrostomia)
  • Genetics: Autosomal dominant, ~1:50,000

Pierre Robin Sequence (1st Arch Syndrome)

Pierre Robin — Triad
  1. Micrognathia — abnormally small mandible
  2. Glossoptosis — posterior displacement of the tongue into the pharynx
  3. Bilateral cleft palate — the tongue physically blocks elevation and midline fusion of the lateral palatine processes

Mechanism: The small mandible forces the tongue to sit posteriorly and superiorly. During the critical window for palatine process elevation (~weeks 7–9), the tongue physically obstructs the lateral palatine processes, preventing their horizontal rotation and midline fusion → bilateral secondary cleft palate.

Genetics: Autosomal recessive. Bony hypoplasia is predominantly maxillary, though the characteristic feature is the small mandible. The sunken chin appearance is characteristic in neonates. Primary concern in the newborn is airway management.

Summary — Prominences & Their Defects
ProminenceNormal ContributionDefect if Fails
Medial nasal (bilateral)Philtrum, primary palate, premaxillaBilateral cleft lip (midline)
Medial nasal + MaxillaryComplete upper lipUnilateral or bilateral cleft lip
Lateral palatine (bilateral)Hard palate posterior to incisive foramen, soft palate, uvulaCleft palate (isolated or with cleft lip)
Lateral nasal + MaxillaryNasolacrimal duct obliterationOblique facial cleft; epiphora

Practice MCQs

Click an option to check your answer, or use "Show Answer" to reveal with explanation.

Question 01
The philtrum of the upper lip is formed by the fusion of which structures?
  • ATwo maxillary prominences
  • BMaxillary and lateral nasal prominences
  • CTwo medial nasal prominences
  • DMandibular and maxillary prominences
The philtrum is formed by the merger of the two medial nasal prominences (which descend from the frontonasal prominence). The skin over this merged segment forms the characteristic vertical groove of the philtrum. Failure of this merger results in a midline cleft.
Question 02
A 28-year-old woman in her first trimester is found to have a fetus with a unilateral cleft lip on ultrasound. What is the underlying embryological defect?
  • AFailure of lateral palatine processes to fuse
  • BFailure of the maxillary prominence to fuse with the medial nasal prominence
  • CFailure of the oronasal membrane to rupture
  • DFailure of the nasal septum to descend
Unilateral cleft lip results from failure of the maxillary prominence to fuse with the medial nasal prominence on one side. The underlying cause is typically hypoplasia of the maxillary prominence, preventing mesenchyme from filling the labial groove. This is distinct from cleft palate, which involves the palatine processes.
Question 03
Isolated cleft palate (without cleft lip) is more common in which sex, and why?
  • AMales; their palatine processes are smaller
  • BMales; testosterone accelerates elevation but reduces fusion strength
  • CFemales; neural crest cell migration is less robust
  • DFemales; their lateral palatine processes fuse approximately 1 week later than males
The lateral palatine processes complete fusion approximately one week later in female embryos compared to male embryos. This extended window increases vulnerability to teratogens and other fusion-disrupting factors, explaining why isolated cleft palate has a higher incidence in females.
Question 04
The incisive foramen is an important anatomical landmark in cleft palate classification because it marks the boundary between:
  • AThe primary palate (anterior) and secondary palate (posterior)
  • BThe hard palate and soft palate
  • CMaxillary and palatine bone contributions
  • DThe soft palate and uvula
The incisive foramen (site of the incisive papilla) marks the junction between the primary palate (derived from fused medial nasal prominences — anterior to foramen) and the secondary palate (derived from lateral palatine processes — posterior to foramen). Clefts anterior to this foramen involve the primary palate; those posterior involve the secondary palate.
Question 05
In Pierre Robin sequence, the bilateral cleft palate is best explained by:
  • ADirect hypoplasia of the maxillary prominences
  • BFailure of the oronasal membrane to rupture
  • CPhysical obstruction of palatine process elevation by a posteriorly displaced tongue
  • DTrisomy 13 causing neural tube defects
In Pierre Robin sequence, micrognathia causes the tongue to be positioned posteriorly and superiorly (glossoptosis). During weeks 7–9, when the lateral palatine processes need to elevate from vertical to horizontal, the tongue physically blocks this elevation and prevents midline fusion, leading to bilateral cleft of the secondary palate.
Question 06
Which of the following teeth are supported by the primary palate?
  • ACanines and premolars
  • BCentral and lateral incisors
  • CMolars only
  • DAll upper teeth
The primary palate (premaxillary segment) forms the premaxilla, which supports the four upper incisors (central and lateral). The secondary palate, derived from the lateral palatine processes, supports the canines, premolars, and molars.
Question 07
Treacher Collins syndrome (mandibulofacial dysostosis) results from:
  • AFailure of the second pharyngeal arch to develop
  • BAutosomal recessive loss of TCOF1 gene
  • CTrisomy 21 causing midface hypoplasia
  • DDefective neural crest cell migration into the first pharyngeal arch (autosomal dominant)
Treacher Collins is caused by defective migration or survival of neural crest cells into the first pharyngeal arch. It is autosomal dominant (TCOF1 gene, encodes treacle protein) and occurs in ~1:50,000 live births. Features affect structures derived from the 1st arch: zygomatic complex, mandibular ramus, ear, and eyelids.
Question 08
The alae of the nose are derived from which embryological prominence?
  • ALateral nasal prominences
  • BMedial nasal prominences
  • CMaxillary prominences
  • DFrontonasal prominence directly (undivided)
The alae (wings) of the nose are formed by the lateral nasal prominences, which form the lateral walls of each nasal pit. The dorsum and apex of the nose come from the medial nasal prominences (and frontonasal prominence). The nasal septum is derived from the fused medial nasal prominences.
Question 09
A mother taking anticonvulsant medication during pregnancy delivers a child with a cleft palate. Which mechanism best explains this teratogenic effect?
  • AInterference with neural tube closure
  • BDirect toxicity to the developing tongue
  • CDisruption of lateral palatine process elevation or mesenchymal fusion
  • DPremature closure of the oronasal membrane
Anticonvulsants such as phenytoin and valproate are associated with increased incidence of cleft palate. They interfere with the critical steps of palatine process elevation and/or fusion — possibly by affecting growth factor signaling or mesenchymal proliferation. The critical window is weeks 7–10. Trisomy 13 is also associated with higher incidence of cleft palate.
Question 10
The nasal septum is derived from which structure, and when does it fuse with the hard palate?
  • AMaxillary prominences; fuses during week 6
  • BFused medial nasal prominences; fuses with the hard palate after week 10
  • CLateral nasal prominences; fuses during week 8
  • DFrontonasal prominence directly; fuses simultaneously with upper lip
The nasal septum grows inferiorly from the fused medial nasal prominences (the inferior extension of the frontonasal prominence). It descends progressively and fuses with the forming hard palate after week 10, completing the separation of the left and right nasal passages. The lateral palatine processes must first fuse with each other and with the primary palate before the septum can meet them.
41H — Development of the Face & Palate — Sources: Moore et al., The Developing Human • Sadler, Langman's Medical Embryology • Lecture transcript
